name: Comparing and Ordering Numbers
whatIsIt: Comparing numbers means finding which is bigger, smaller, or whether they are equal. The place-value rule makes it easy: FIRST count the digits — more digits wins. If the digit counts match, compare from the LEFT, place by place, until one digit is bigger.
whyNeeded: Which team scored more? Which toy costs less? Which bottle holds more? Life asks 'which is bigger?' every single day. Comparing correctly needs place value — otherwise 9 looks bigger than 10, and Ravi picks the smaller pile again!
realLifeUses: Shopping: is the ₹95 toy cheaper than the ₹109 toy?
Cricket: 178 runs vs 87 runs — who won?
Heights: 132 cm vs 129 cm — who is taller?
Games: comparing scores to find the winner

vocabulary.meaning: The open mouth always eats the bigger number: 109 > 91.
The point aims at the smaller number: 91 < 109.

story.title: Which Pile Is Bigger?
story.text: At the shop, Meena compares two heaps of tamarind — one marked 91, the other 109. 'The 9 in 91 looks so big — is 91 more?' 'Count the DIGITS first,' says the shopkeeper. '109 has three digits; 91 has only two. More digits means a bigger number, no matter how big a single digit looks. So 109 is greater.'
story.answerInStory: 109 — it has three digits while 91 has only two. More digits means a bigger number, no matter how big the digits look!

visual.caption: 109 owns a hundred-flat; 91 has only ten-rods. One hundred beats nine tens — 109 > 91.

teachingGallery.title: Compare from the left
teachingGallery.note: Line numbers up by place. The first place that differs decides it.

examples.caption: 3 hundreds < 5 hundreds → 358 < 512. Decide at the FIRST digit.
7 hundreds > 2 hundreds → 721 > 299, even though 299 has big tens/ones.
On the number line, numbers to the right are bigger.

standardMethod.summary: Count digits first; if equal, compare place by place from the LEFT.
steps: STEP 1 — Count the digits. More digits = bigger number (109 > 91).
STEP 2 — Same digit count? Compare the LEFTMOST digits (372 vs 359: 3 = 3, move on).
STEP 3 — Keep moving right until one digit is bigger (7 > 5, so 372 > 359).
STEP 4 — Write it with the hungry mouth eating the bigger number: 372 > 359.

alternateMethods.name: Blocks method
alternateMethods.whenToUse: To SEE why one number is bigger.
steps: Build both numbers with place-value blocks.
Compare the biggest blocks first: hundreds, then tens, then ones.
The number with more of the biggest block wins immediately.
alternateMethods.example: 109 has a hundred-flat; 91 has none — 109 wins before even counting tens!

alternateMethods.name: Number line method
steps: Picture the numbers on a number line.
Read them left to right for ascending order.
alternateMethods.example: 128, 218, 281 sit left to right on the line — that is ascending order.

steps: Digit count: both have 3 digits — go to place-by-place.
372 > 359 (the ones never even got a turn).

workedExamples.problem: Ravi says 19 > 21 'because 9 is the biggest digit on the board.' Set him straight.
steps: Both have 2 digits, so compare from the LEFT — not the biggest digit anywhere!
21 > 19. The big 9 sits in the ones room — it only counts as nine.
Left digits rule; loud digits don't!
workedExamples.answer: 21 > 19 — compare from the left, not by the flashiest digit.

trickPractice.intro: Don't read the whole number — the first place that differs already decides it.

hintLadder: Line them up by place. Compare the LEFT-most digit first.
If the left digits are equal, move one place right.
The number with the bigger digit at the first difference wins.

The number with the bigger digit at the first difference wins.

The number with the bigger digit at the first difference wins.
Line them up by place. Compare the LEFT-most digit first.
If the left digits are equal, move one place right.
The number with the bigger digit at the first difference wins.

commonMistakes.mistake: Choosing 91 over 109 because 9 looks bigger than 1 — forgetting to count digits first.
commonMistakes.fix: Always count digits FIRST. A 3-digit number beats any 2-digit number — a small general with a bigger army wins!

commonMistakes.mistake: Comparing from the ones (right side) instead of the left: picking 359 over 372 because 9 > 2.
commonMistakes.fix: Start at the LEFT — the biggest place. The first place where the digits differ decides everything; the smaller places can't overturn it.

commonMistakes.mistake: Thinking the number containing the biggest single digit wins (19 > 21 'because of the 9').
commonMistakes.fix: A digit's power depends on its PLACE. A 9 in the ones room is just nine; a 2 in the tens room is twenty. Compare places, not show-off digits.

hintLadder: Count the digits in each number first.
109 has 3 digits; 91 has 2.
More digits always wins — like Captain Count's bridge!
easy.explain: 3-digit numbers start at 100, which is already past every 2-digit number.

hintLadder: Both have 2 digits — compare from the LEFT.
Tens: 4 vs 5 — which is bigger?
5 tens beat 4 tens, so 54 wins.
Same digit count — start at the LEFT.
Hundreds tie (3 = 3). Move to the tens.
7 tens vs 5 tens — decided!

medium.q: Type the SMALLEST of these numbers: 218, 281, 128
hintLadder: All have 3 digits — compare hundreds first.
Hundreds digits: 2, 2, 1. Which is smallest?
The number starting with 1 hundred is smallest.

challenge.q: Ravi says 19 > 21 'because 9 is the biggest digit.' What is the real rule?

options.label: Compare from the LEFT place — 2 tens beat 1 ten, so 21 > 19
He is right — the biggest digit wins
Count the digits — 21 has more digits

challenge.answer: Compare from the LEFT place — 2 tens beat 1 ten, so 21 > 19
hintLadder: Both numbers have TWO digits, so digit count ties.
Where does the mighty 9 stand — tens or ones?
A 9 in the ones room is only nine. Compare the tens first.

challenge.q: Using each of the digits 4, 0, 9 exactly once, type the LARGEST 3-digit number you can make.
hintLadder: The biggest digit should grab the biggest room — the hundreds.
Then the next biggest digit takes the tens.
9 hundreds, 4 tens, 0 ones.
challenge.explain: Put the biggest digit in the biggest place: 940.

hintLadder: Count digits first!
178 has 3 digits, 87 has 2.
Three-digit beats two-digit, always.
Same digits count — compare from the LEFT.
Hundreds tie (6 = 6). Compare tens.
4 tens vs 3 tens.
Hundreds all tie at 3 — compare the tens.
Tens digits: 5, 6, 3.
The 6 in the tens room wins.

questions.q: When two numbers have the SAME number of digits, where do you start comparing?

options.label: From the LEFT — the biggest place first
From the ones — the right side

questions.answer: From the LEFT — the biggest place first
hintLadder: Which place is worth the most?
Hundreds beat tens beat ones.
So start where the value is biggest — the left.

teachBackPrompt: You are the new bridge keeper! Teach someone at home the two comparing rules — count digits first, then compare from the left — and show them with 91 vs 109 why Ravi nearly fell off the bridge.

revisionCard.summary: Rule 1: count digits — more digits wins (109 > 91). Rule 2: same count → compare from the LEFT, place by place (372 > 359 because 7 tens > 5 tens). The hungry mouth > always eats the bigger number!

gameMission.title: The More-or-Less Bridge
gameMission.brief: Cross the wobbling bridge by always stepping to the bigger number! Digit-count traps, tricky nines, and Ravi's loud wrong answers await. Ten safe steps and the Jungle is yours.

realLifeProject: Collect 4 prices from home (biscuit packet, toothpaste, a book, anything). Write them on slips, shuffle, and arrange them from cheapest to costliest — ascending order! Then answer: which two prices were hardest to compare, and which rule decided it?

rememberIt.hook: Compare from the LEFT: more digits wins; same length, go digit by digit
rememberIt.unpack: > and < point to the SMALLER number. 'The crocodile mouth eats the bigger number.'

studentQuestions.a: Same length, so left to right: hundreds equal, tens 5 vs 6 -> 460 is bigger.
The wide open mouth faces the BIGGER number. 7 > 3.
